Do you find enrichment APIs to be useful like the zoom info? I clear a bit. >> Enrichment is a very uh common use case um on dreamer. Any application on dreamer can kick off a sub agent to do a particular task. Um so this actually is a powerful agentic harness that runs inside of its own VM. We call them sidekick tasks because they actually run in the context of the sidekick. I'll talk more about sidekick in a second. And enrichment is a very common use case. And the cool thing about a sidekick task is that it has access to all the tools in the platform but also public data as well. And so very frequently enrichment on our platform happens using public data that it can be found in the web. There are some tools for getting people data uh from uh from various bespoke systems and so that works pretty well. But actually you'd be

surprised. I mean we would love if someone out there would like to build a zoom info tool, we don't have one today. We'd love to see that on the platform and I'm sure it will be very powerful. But we're also seeing that this powerful agentic harness can pull a lot of data in. You on that note of tools that make experiences better. We're constantly adding more tools because people in the community are building them and publishing them. We review the tools carefully and then they go live for everybody. Please do. And you'll even find some in the gallery that you can remix. So, he was saying this morning that he's he built this self-completing to-do list uh on dreamer already, but he connected the granola tool yesterday and now something really magical happens, which is when he says in meetings that he's going to do a thing, it magically shows up on his to-do list and then it can magically get completed and then as I mentioned all the agents, all the apps on dreamer can actually work together. So our coding agent as it builds them does something very special where it exposes the internals of each of the experiences to the system and then sidekick can manipulate those to get stuff done. So he has built another agent which he uses for

recruiting. It kind of keeps track of candidates and also it's got a kind of mini CRM function so he's able to introduce candidates to each other. He told us this morning that something he committed to do in a meeting that was recorded on Granola yesterday showed up on his magic to-do list and his magic to-do list, it was like introduce a person for recruiting, used his recruiting agent to get it done. >> Um, and this is this is the dream. This is why we started the company. It really is the case that you can build and use these very powerful bespoke experiences that can automate your life by working together. And I'd love to talk a little bit about how they work together. So obviously it's really cool to have software that will work on your behalf, but it's only useful if you can trust it, right? So privacy and security is very important to us. Making these things accessible and useful while also being trustworthy is hard. So the model that we have which is working very well is that the sidekick is at the core of everything here. So it is both your companion your helper but it's also the traffic cop in the system. So when when one agent wants to work with another agent in dreamer, it doesn't do it directly. It does it via the sidekick. Ask the sidekick to do the thing and the sidekick understands both everything all the expectations that have been set with me as a user about what agents can do, which tools I've given them permission to use. And it will make sure that whatever is is going on is actually aligned with my own interests. And you know that's part of the background that I bring to this problem domain. I've worked for years uh keeping very important information safe and secure. And so as we started to think about this problem, we realized that we actually had to build something that's a bit like an operating system. You know, the sidekick's like the kernel, the agents and apps are like user different rings. >> Exactly. Because if you try to pick off just one piece of this, you can't actually make it work for people at scale. Uh because you could build little vibecoded apps, but they're going to grab all your

data willy-nilly. They won't be able to work together. you actually have to invest in the fundamental core in order to make it work well for people and that's what we've been doing and it's uh it's been a lot of fun. One other thing I wanted to mention is um I've obviously talked about two things tools and aentic apps. We really designed Dreamer to be an ecosystem and a platform. And one of my favorite quotes about platforms. I think it's from Bill Gates is that you can only be a platform if you create more value for the folks participating and using the platform than than the platform itself creates. And that's our goal here. So we at every step have been thinking about how do we make sure that other people are deriving even more value from dreamer than we are. So in that vein, I already mentioned tool builders get paid and people can build agents that solve their needs and share them with others. The sidekick gets to know you and it builds up a memory about you over time and that turns out to be very important. So, dreamer. >> Dreamer gets better the more you use it. For instance, a lot of agents in the platform when you start using them, the first thing they do is show you, here's what I think is relevant to you for this particular use case, uh, a very popular kind of agent on Dreamer is a weekend activity planner. the week to figure this out. So, there is an agent on Dreamer called Weekend Activity Planner and it helps me find things to do with with the family at the weekend. In fact, this morning I got a message from my weekend activity planner telling me about the St. Patrick's Day parade on Saturday at Civic Center. I'm Irish. My kids are technically Irish as well. I mean, they they have multiple citizenships, but you know, they're they're Irish. Um, what a better thing to do than take them to the St. Patrick's Day parade. Why did that get recommended to me? Because in the profile that weekend activity planner knows about me, it knows that I'm Irish, right? So all of that comes from the memory that sidekick builds up about me over time. We have invested in this a bunch. We will continue to invest in this more. We've tried actually many different techniques. As you know, the the kind of um cutting edge of aentic memory has changed over time. you know, very early on we were putting lots of facts into a vector database and uh and doing embeddings and pulling them back out um using, you know, reverse lookup of embeddings rag that actually worked but turned out to be much more complexity than was actually required. So, you know, today we've replaced it with a different system. Uh I think we use a system that's pretty similar to what you'll find in lots of other products, but it's an area that we're actively uh investing in. Like there's there's more than one person at the company specifically working on memory. And so expect us to just continue to make it better. >> Did you try knowledge graphs? >> We've tried knowledge graphs. The system that we have now is not a knowledge graph. Everyone cares about getting to know everybody else and sharing what they're doing with everybody else. And that's great. I'm not saying they shouldn't, right? The very like I want to work in teams that are fun where people are talking to each other and and sharing ideas and so forth. But,

you know, there's just a kind of gravitational weight that comes from larger and larger teams. So, just inherently large organizations are less nimble than small ones. And if you run a large organization, you have to keep thinking about how do I kind of like prune things so that it can act like a small team. So, one of the main things that I look for now when hiring engineers is how well do you work with coding agents? Our team actually is quite experienced. And um people ask me this question a lot because it's like very counterintuitive. Wouldn't it be much easier to use the web views that are on the operating system? Wouldn't it be much easier not to have to do that? And the answer is yes. And like obviously I did that once upon a time. I did that. There was a version of the Slack app that used just the operating system that we use. Wait, did you did you start the Slack app? >> I would Well, team effort in Yeah, but I was I was there and we built the Slack app. Yeah, >> it's crazy. Um I mean obviously get the Electron guy to do it, but >> Well, but this is an interesting point. Like by the time by the time I joined Slack, they already had an app that was built with something at the time called Macap. It was a little bit like the same app thing for mobile. it just used the operating systems web views. Um, and that didn't work for like so many reasons. Um, and they were like, "All right, maybe we need like bigger guns. We need like take more control of the rendering stack." And there's there's a few things I always mention here. Um, I think if you're building a small app, just going with the operating systems, spread view is perfectly fine. If you're building an app maybe that doesn't have too many users who will like cry bloody murder if it doesn't work, that is fine. The reason to go with your own embedded rendering engine is because, and this is still true in 2026, the operating system rendering engines are not that good. They're just not that good. Both Microsoft and

Apple are trying to move away from that. They so far really haven't. The only way to upgrade those is to upgrade your operating system. So, if you're say a Slack and you have a critical rendering bug in WK Webb and some of the other web view options, your only recourse is to tell your customer, "Oh, sorry, you're too poor. You didn't buy the latest MacBook. Unacceptable. >> Unacceptable to user. Unacceptable as a developer. So, you sort of need to like go down the stack and like find the best rendering engine, then put it in your app. Why Chromium? Even though it's very big, Chromium is by far the best thing. Like I I often like to remind people the Unreal Engine. You want to render some text, they use Chromium. Like Chromium is part of the Unreal Engine for same purposes. Chromium is very very good. I think it's like one of the marvels of engineering. It's very hard from we're in San Francisco right now as we're recording. Most of the people in the city are web developers. It's hard for me to like overstate how magical it is. autoencoder framework um they show that there's a lot of benefit from getting the temporal compression which makes a lot of sense as uh the each sequential frames and videos have mostly redundant information um so by compressing against compressing in the temporal space you allow the latent to hold a lot more semantic information while uh avoiding that duplicate um so we've got our SpaceTime latence possibly but via there's some 3D vae presumably a MAG vat V2 um and then you throw it into a diffusion Transformer so um I I think it's personally interesting to note that op Sora is using a MAG vv2 which originally used an autoaggressive Transformer decoder to model the latent space but uh is now using a diffusion uh diffusion Transformer so it's still a Transformer happening just the question is like is it parameterizing the stochastic uh differential equation is it parameterizing a conditional distribution via Auto regression um it's ALS um it's also worth noting that most to evaluate um uh open models uh but um in general like if you are if you care a lot about intervention to do on this model which is my uh prefer area of of research then you know the resources that you need um are quite quite significant um one of trend um that has emerged in 2024 is this cluster of um fully open models um Soo uh the model that we built at ai2 being one of them um and you know it's nice that it's not just us there's like a cluster of other mostly research um efforts who are working on this um and so it's good to um to give you a primer of what like fully open means um so fully open the easy way to think about it is instead of just releasing a model check point that you run you release a full recipe so that um other people working on it uh working on that space can pick and choose whatever they want from your recipe and create their own model or improve on top of your own model um you're giving out the full Pipeline and all the details there um instead of just like the end output um so I pull up the screenshot from our recent ume model um and like for this model for example we the model itself the data that was stain on the code both for training and inference um all the logs that we got through um the training run as well as um every intermediate checkpoint um and like this fact the fact that you release different part of pipeline uh allows others to do really cool things um so for example this tweet from early this year from uh folks and news research um they use our pre-training data uh to do a replication of the bitnet paper in the open um so they took just a really like the initial part of a pipeline um and then did the thing on top of it um it goes both ways so for example for the Alo 2 model um a lot of our pre-training data for the first stage of pre-training um was from this dclm uh initiative uh that was led by folks uh o a variety of in VAR institution it was a really nice group effort but

um you know for when it was nice to be able to say okay you know the state-ofthe-art in terms of like what is done in the open has improved we don't have to like do all this work from scratch to catch up the state-ofthe-art we can just take it directly and integrate it and do our own improvements on top of that um I'm going to spend a few minutes uh talk doing like a shamless plug for uh some of our fully open recipes um so indulge me in this um so a few things that we released this year was as I was mentioning this omo model um which is I think still is state-of-the-art um Moe model in its size class um and it's also fully open so every component of of this model available um we release a multimodel model called MMO um MMO is not just a model but it's a full recipe of how you go from a tax only model to a multimodal model and we appli this recipe on top of quen checkpoints on top of Almo checkpoints as well as on top ofo um and I think they've been replication doing that on top of mistol as well um um on on the post training side uh we recently released Tulu 3 um same story this is a recipe on how you go from a base model to um a state-of-the-art post training model uh we use the Tulu recipe on top of Almo on top of llama and then is been um open replication effort to do that on top of quen as well uh it's really nice to see like you know when your recipe sort of it's kind of Turn Key you can apply it to different models and it kind of just works um and finally the last thing we released this here waso 2 um which so far is the best state of the AR uh fully open language model um it sort of combines aspect from all three of these previous models um what we learn on the data side from omoi and what we learn on like making models that are easy to adapt from the Mulo project and the Tulu project um I will close with a little bit of reflection like way is this this ecosystem of open models um

we're trying to be what you'd feel an elite devops team would be so here's kind of a a feature list of some of the stuff we're going for and and this is the vibe that we're going for so um most people like we actually started out uh optimizing well we started out building compute Hardware cooling our own uh Power Systems like that then we got into optimizing training time so we're messing around with Cuda kernels and data

loading and that kind of stuff and we thought all right this is the hard part this is the rocket science like getting these You Know Much Greater efficiencies on the GPU surely the easy part is just taking our work once it's done and just putting it on a cloud platform and having it Go and it turned out to to be the complete opposite we got a whole bunch of awesome optimizations done in a few months and then it took us a couple of years to actually build a GPU orchestration platform that that we wanted to use and what we realized was that there was just a lot of things that that you needed to solve um what I want to share with you today is something we've been working on for a year which is uh a new UI for how you uh work with clusters um and so this is it here um so if you've got a uh you know some compute maybe AWS has given you some credits that's really nice of them so you've got a cluster over in uh Us East um maybe you've already got some stuff with uh with gcp um maybe you've got some on Prem as well and in each of those regions you've got uh you know some number of gpus um and then you want to go and do things with them you want to go train train models on them so you uh uh the the data set stuff I'll I'll get to in a sec but um uh you you know you go and run a job right and that job's going to start on some cluster somewhere so with our system it's pretty much that easy you don't need to worry about Linux and video drivers anything like that you get a Docker you get root in the docker it can just jump on the cluster straight away and so then a bunch of other people want to run jobs and so then you end up backed up in a slurm que and you go hang on don't we have all this other compute somewhere else in the world uh what's it going to take to actually migrate our workloads to those other resources well what we've built is the ability to migrate those workloads like this right and what that

means is that we can uh snapshot the state of a cluster live um we can move the entire cluster workload to another cloud provider um or another region or another cluster on that cloud provider um we can do that in ibly quickly as well I've got a little bit of a video showing some of our speed demos um so uh we rewrote file transfer block storage like absolutely everything that number is going to be a lot higher that 90 gbes a second soon um and we have the world's fastest container registry as well so um yeah there's no more no more slow stuff on the cluster um it's also a lot cheaper than what you'd be used to paying for egress so the vision for this is if if you imagine a a CPU and it's there's a scheduler and it's sending workloads out to cores on that CPU well what if the whole world was the CPU what if each core was actually a cluster in a Data Center and you just felt like your computer had incredible amounts of power and obviously you know theoretically that's possible but it's all about the apis and the interface work normally you know you want to go and um you know start sending workloads around the world you've got to go and talk to you know the Dozen Dev Ops people and they'll get you know started on a multi-week multi-month project to do that um the these transfers happen incredibly quickly and without the need for for any devops work to do um we've also got a few other features here as well so you know you you'll have some data sets and um they might be quite large so um maybe you've got you know a 10 uh terabyte data set and uh we'll pop that in the US to start with um we can oh name's required all right 10 just lose the screen happen there things it's still on yeah there we go um so I'll just pop a 10 terab data set in so I can go pull that down to any cluster I want um and then I can go and you know I can do training on that data set but I can also set up workstations so one of the issues that we've seen people encounter is you

know yeah I can get agpu reasonably easily but it's not going to have highspeed access to my data set um so here we can just you know set up as many nodes as we want with workstations you can carve off as many gpus as you'd like and that'll be your you know your container that you can work out of um and have highspeed access that's that 90 gigabyte a second access to that data set um and uh that way you can go so this is the entire Dev cycle we're not doing any you know production inference hosting right now but you can have you know fast access to your data sets from your Dev container you can train on the Clusters very very easily um what we want to do is eliminate any developer waiting time or any work that wasn't P torch so uh what does that look like for some other examples um we can also because we're able to save the state of a cluster we're resilient to things like GPU failure but we can also do this uh this trick where we go and build a Time cycled space and here we can actually just go and pack as many jobs as we want into the same space on a cluster and choose how often they're going to rotate so maybe it's every 3 hours every job gets an hour every 3 hours maybe this job's more important maybe this job's so important and our cluster is so backed up that we just actually want to get this job some dedicated space so we hit burst and we spin it up and now it's actually got we've actually gone and found resources on the cloud so we're integrated with six clouds um that's growing um and you can plug your own cloud accounts in you can use ours whatever you like we're just a a management platform um and and that those time cycled spaces uh one of the really cool things about them is that uh what they can let you do is um they can uh you can interrupt them anytime you want so if you want to launch a cluster scale workload it's usually about 15 seconds to start once so you you Dev on a GPU you want you know a cluster to work on will'll get

you on for 15 in 15 seconds you'll you know see if your stuff works at cluster scale you'll get off all the other jobs will will keep going with that state saving so we're trying to make GPU feel very fluid um so this is our our platform uh we're running events uh about every month um there's one coming up uh this jump to it um yeah this uh Friday and Saturday for any folks who are heading home early from Europe um you get to play with our platform there um we do just let people to train chess models on it uh last event we got up to 1,600 gpus people were really having a lot of fun with it um and so that's uh that's what's happening um this Friday and and every month or so I don't know if I can yeah I saw this hackathon and I was wondering what the expected ELO of a chess bot that you can train in a day is you know that was uh we were curious about that as well so this is the sixth time sixth time we've run this the the first time you know your you know child who's just learned the rules of Chess could probably defeat most of the models um but around the the fourth time we'd run it I think they were probably getting up around sort of 1500 ELO um people were really getting a lot better at at figuring out how to do this stuff in a weekend um and uh yeah I think um you know I'm not the world's greatest chess player and uh right now the models will quite easily defeat me um and uh yeah we're we're you know excited to see how much further this will go um uh so this is like a short form way to access the platform um we also have research grants um so if you want access to compute we've given out about a dozen of these so far and um we'll give you like you know 10 to 100K worth of compute and you just have to be doing something cool something interesting something that uh swix would want to host um at an event and uh yeah you can come and get some compute so we're uh we'll give you like about three months

uh on the systems with that as well be and but otherwise I just want to let people mingle you always have uh we have AWS gcp azour Oracle oblivous Lambda Labs our own metal and we've got some more on the way um it's the the same like the the way our systems work is we just got a a buntu image that has our agent in it it's called Agent Smith um and it has our uh fast data puller our sort of node clustering stuff a whole bunch of Health monitoring um yeah some of the stuff I didn't show you today like you get total Financial control you can set budgets across all providers you go all right this developer allowed 10K a month or this projects allowed 100K it'll just T up all the usage and then you know tell them when they need to stop so yeah it's uh it's very very multioud yes can you repeat the question uh yeah so the question was how we compare this with something like modal so so modal is a great environment where you want um you know probably not cluster scale um so a smaller number of gpus and where you're willing to sort of convert a lot of your workflow to the way that that model operates what we've tried to do is make it so it's it's we're sort of clusters first and foros like yes you can have workstations but it's really about larger clusters and making the data extremely fast so that those you're not sitting around waiting for for days for that um the other thing is we've tried to make it as close to how you operate now as possible so um you know Docker is you know for it is it is the standard um we didn't use Docker to start with um because we were concerned about the speed we have since applied a bunch of our speed tooling to it and it's only about a 5% overhead at the moment and so you get on our system you get a Docker container you get root in the container you can do anything except install kernel modules so you get a lot of freedom um and you don't have to convert to another way of doing things cool who's yeah see okay um cool well thank you

using synthetic data for pre-training has become really popular after the textbooks are all you need papers um

where Microsoft basically trained a series of small models on textbooks that were using uh uh a large llm and then they found that these models were actually better than models that are much larger so this was really interesting it was like first of its time but it was also met with a lot of skepticism which is a good thing in research it pushes you to question things um because the data set that weights of the models and hugging face because we like open source we try to reproduce what they did so this is our cosmop data set we basically tried to follow a similar approach to what they documented in the paper and we created a synthetic data set of textbooks and blog posts and stories that had almost 30 billion tokens and we try trained some models on that and and um we found that like the key ingredient to getting a good data set that is synthetic is trying as much as possible to keep it diverse because if you just throw the same prompts as your model like generate like a textbook about linear algebra and even if you change the temperature the textbooks are going to look alike so there's no way you could scale to like millions of samples and the way you do that is by creating prompts that have some seeds that make them diverse um in our case The Prompt we we would ask the model to generate textbook but make it related to an extract from a web page and also we try to frame it within to stay within topic for example here we put like an extract about cardiovascular bioimaging and then we ask the model to generate a textbook related to medicine that is also related to this web page and this is a really nice approach because there's so many web pages out there so you can be sure that your Generations is not got to be diverse when you change uh the seed example um one thing that's challenging with this is that you want the seed samples to be related to your

topics so we use like a Search tool to try to go all of fine web data set and find the pages that are related to the topics we're interested in and then we also do a lot of experiments with the type of generations we want the model to generate for example we ask it for textbooks for middle school students or textbook for college and we found that like some generation Styles help on some specific benchmarks While others help on other benchmarks for example College textbook books are really good for mlu where Middle School textbooks are good for benchmarks like open book QA and P um this is uh some samples from C4 data sets and then they use an llm to

rewrite these samples into a better format for example they ask an llm to rewrite the sample into a Wikipedia passage or into a Q&A page and uh the interesting thing in this approach is that you can use a model that is small because it doesn't rewriting doesn't require knowledge it's just rewriting a page into a different style so the model doesn't need to have like knowledge that is like extensive of what is rewriting compared to just asking a model to generate a new textbook and not giving it like ground truth um so here they re rewrite some samples from C4 into Q&A each Wikipedia and they find that doing this works better than training just on C4 and so what they did in neotron cc is a similar approach uh they rewrite some pages from common craw uh for two reasons one is to like uh improve uh pages that are low quality so they rewrite them into for example a Wikipedia page so they look better and another reason is to create more diverse data sets so they have a data set that they already heavily filtered and then they take these pages that are all the high quality and they ask the model to rewrite them in question and answer format into like open-ended questions or like multi choice questions so this way they can reuse the same page multiple times without fearing like having multiple duplicates because it's the same information but it's going to be written differently so I think that's also a really interesting approach for like um generating synthetic data just by rephrasing the pages that you already have uh there's also this approach uh called procs where U they try to start from a web page and then they generate a program which finds how to rewrite that page to make it better and less moisy for example here you can see that there's some leftover metadata in the web page and you don't necessarily want to keep that for training your model so they train model that can generate programs that can like normalize and remove lines that are extra so I think this approach is also interesting but it's maybe less scalable than the approaches that I presented before um so that was it for like rephrasing and generating new textbooks another approach that I think

is really good and becoming really popular for using synthetic data for pre-training is basically building better classifiers for filtering the web uh for example here we released the data set called Fine web edu and the way we built it is by taking Lama 3 and asking it to rate the educational content of web pages from zero to five so for example if a page is like a really good textbook that could be useful in a school setting it would get really high score and if a page is just like an advertisement or promotional material it would get a lower score and then after that we take these synthetic annotations and we train a classifier on them uh it's a classifier like a birth model and then we run this classifier on all of fine web which is a 15 trillion tokens data set and then we only keep the pages that have like a score that's higher than three so for example in our case we went from 15 trillion tokens to just 1.5 trillion tokens that are really highly educational and as you can see here fine web edu outperforms all the other uh public web data sets by a large margin on a couple of benchmarks here I show the aggregated score and you can see that this approach is really effective for filtering web data sets to get like better corpuses for training your llms um others also try to do this approach there's for example the dclm data set uh where they also train the classifier but not to detect educational content instead they trained it on open hermis data set which is a data set for instruction tuning and also they explain like IM5 subredits and then they also get really high quality uh data set uh which is like a very information L and can help you train some really good LMS um and the neotron come CW they also did this approach but instead of using one classifier they used an ensemble of classifiers so they use for example the dclm classifier and also classifiers like the ones we used in fine web educational and then they combine these scores into with an ensemble method to only retain the best high quality pages and they get a data set that works even better than the ones with development

and it's uh Earth software development agents it's pretty impressive um the next thing I'd like to talk about a little bit is things I worry about when designing agents so we're designing agents to you know do a very difficult task of like navigating websites um writing code other things like this and within 2024 there's been like a huge Improvement in the methodology that we use to do this um but there's a bunch of things we think about there's a bunch of interesting papers and I'd like to introduce a few of them so the first thing I worry about is um the agent computer interface like how do we get an agent to interact with computers and um how do we provide agents with the tools to do the job and within Open Hands um we are doing the thing on the right but there's also a lot of agents that do the thing on the left so the thing on the left is you give like agents kind of granular tools you give them tools like um or let's say your instruction is I want to determine the most cost-effective country to purchase the smartphone model Cod act one uh the countries to consider are the USA Japan Germany and India and you have a bunch of available apis and so what you do for some agents is you provide them all of these tools apis as tools that they can call and so in this particular case um in order to solve this problem you'd have to make about like 30 tool calls right you'd have to call look up rates for um Germany you'd have to look it up for the US Japan and India that's four tool goals and then you go through and do all of these things um separately and the method that we adopt in Open Hands instead is we provide these tools but we provide them by just giving a coding agent the ability to call arbitrary python code um and in the arbitrary python code it can call these tools we expose these tools as apis

that the model can call and what that allows us to do is instead of writing 20 tool calls making 20 llm calls you write a program that runs all of these all at once and it gets the result and of course it can execute that program it can you know make a mistake it can get errors back and and fix things um but that makes our job a lot easier and this has been really like instrumental to Our Success I think um another part of this is what tools does the agent need and I I think this depends on your use case we're kind of extreme um and we're only giving the agent five tools um or maybe six tools and what what are they uh the first one is program execution so it can execute bash programs and it can execute uh Jupiter notebooks it can execute cells in Jupiter notebooks um so that those are two tools um another one is a file editing tool um and the file editing tool allows you to browse parts of files um and uh kind of read them overwrite them other stuff like this and then we have another Global search and replace tool so it's actually two tools for file editing and then a final one is web browsing web browsing I'm kind of cheating when I call it only one tool you actually have like scroll and text input and in Click and other stuff like that but um these are basically the only things we allow the agent to do um what then the question is like what if we want it to allow finding all

of the you know comments on your issues or checking GitHub actions and stuff um the second thing I think about is the human agent interface so this is like how do we get humans to interact with agents um I already showed you one variety of our human agent interface it's basically a chat window where you can browse through the agents results and things like this um this is very very difficult um I I don't think anybody has a good answer to this and I don't think we have a good answer to this but um the the guiding principles that I'm trying to follow are we want to present enough info to the user so we want to uh present them with you know what the agent is uh doing in the form of a kind of English description so you can see here um you can see here every time it takes an action it says like I will help you create a script for sending emails um when it runs A bash command um sorry that's a little small when it runs A bash command it will um say ran a bash command it won't actually show you the whole bash command or the whole jupyter notebook because it can be really large but you can open it up and see um if you want to by clicking on this so like um if you want to explore more you can click over to the jupyter notebook and and see what's displayed in the Jupiter notebook and you get like lots and lots of information um so that's one thing um another thing is go where the user is so like if the user's already interacting in a particular setting uh then I'd like to you know integrate into that setting but only to a point um so it open hands we have a chat UI for interaction we have a GitHub plugin for tagging and resolving issues isues so basically what you do is you um do at open hands agent and the Open Hands agent will like see that comment and be able to go in and fix things so if you say at Open Hands agent uh tests are failing on this PR please fix the tests it will go in and fix the tests for you and stuff like this

um another thing we have is a remote uh runtime for launching headless jobs so if you want to launch like a fleet of agents to solve you know uh five different problems at once you can also do that through an AP so we have um we have these interfaces and this probably depends on the use case so like depending if you're a coding agent you want to do things one way if you're a like Insurance auditing agent you'll want to do things other ways obviously um another thing I think about a lot is choosing a language model um and for agentic LMS uh we have to have a bunch of things work really well um the first thing is really really good instruction following ability and if you have really good instruction following ability it opens up like a ton of possible applications for you um tool use and coding abilities so if you provide tools it needs to be able to use them well environment understanding so um it needs like if you're building a web agent it needs to be able to understand web pages either through uh Vision or through text um and error awareness and Recovery ability so if it makes a mistake it needs to be able to you know figure out why it made a mistake come up with alternative strategies and other things like this um under the hood in all of the demos that I did now um Claude we're using Claude um Claude has all of these abilities uh very good not perfect but very good um most others don't have these abilities quite as much so like GPD 40 doesn't have very good uh Air recovery ability and so because of this it will go into loops and do the same thing over and over and over again whereas Claude does not do this um Claud if you if you use the agents enough you get used to their kind of like personality and Claude says hm let me try a different approach uh a lot so you know obviously it's been trained in some way to you know elicit this ability um we did an evaluation this is old um and we need to update this basically but um we evaluated Claud GPD 401 mini um Lama 405b deeps 2.5 on being a good

code agent within our framework and Claude was kind of Head and Shoulders Above the Rest um GPD 40 was kind of okay the best open source model was llama 3.1 405b um this needs to be updated because this is like a few months old by now and you know things are moving really really fast but I still am under the impression that CLA is the best the other closed models are you know not quite as good and then uh the open model are a little bit behind that gr I we haven't tried gr at all actually so it's a good question if you want to try it um I'd be happy to help yeah cool another thing is planning um and so there's a few considerations for planning um the first one is whether you have a curated plan or you have it generated on the Fly and so for solving GitHub issues you can kind of have uh overall plan like the plan is um first reproduce if there's an issue first write tests to reproduce the issue or to demonstrate the issue after that run the tests and make sure they fail then go in and fix the tests run the tests again to make sure they pass and then you're done so that's like a pretty good workflow for like solving uh coding issues and you could curate that ahead of time um another option is to let the language model basically generate its own plan um and both of these are are perfectly valid um another one is explicit structure versus implicit structure so let's say you generate a plan um if you have explicit structure you could like write a multi-agent system and the multi-agent system would have your uh reproducer agent and then it would have your um your bug uh your test writer agent and your bug fixer agent and uh lots of different agents and you would explicitly write this all out in code and then uh then use it that way on the other hand you could just provide a prompt that says please do all of these things in order um so in Open Hands we do very light planning um we have a single prompt we don't have any multi-agent systems um but we do provide like instructions about like what to do first

what to do next and other things like this um I'm not against uh doing it the other way but I laid out some uh kind of justification for this in this blog called don't sleep on single agent systems and the basic idea behind this is if you have a really really good instruction following agent um it will follow the instructions as long as things are working according to your plan but let's say you need to deviate from your plan you still have the flexibility to do this and if you do explicit structure through a multi-agent system it becomes a lot harder to do that like you get stuck um when things deviate from your plan um there's also uh some other examples and I wanted to introduce a few papers uh so one paper I liked recently is this paper called coact um where you generate plans and then go in and fix them um and so the basic idea is like if you need to deviate from your plan you can uh you know figure out that your plan was not working and go back and deviate from it another thing I think about a lot is specifying common workflows so we're trying to tackle uh software development and I already showed like three use cases where um we do software development and uh when we do software development we do a ton of different things but we do them over and over and over again so just to of an example um we fix GitHub actions when GitHub actions are failing and we do that over and over and over again that's not the number one thing that software Engineers do but it's a you know high up on the list so how can we get a list of all of like the workflows that people are are working on and there's a few research works that people have done in this direction um one example is manual prompting so there's this nice paper called Step that got state-ofthe-art on the web Arena web navigation Benchmark where they came up with a bunch of manual workflows for solving different web navigation tasks and we also have a paper recently called Agent workflow memory where the B basic idea behind this is we want to create self-improving agents that learn from the past their

past successes and the way it works is we have a memory that has an example of lots of the previous workflows that people have used and every time the agent finishes a task and itself judges that it did a good job at that task you take that task you break it down into individual workflows included in that and then you put it back in the prompts for the agent to work next time and this um we demonstrated that this leads to um a 22.5% increase on web Arina um after uh 40 examples so that's a pretty you know huge increase by kind of self-learning and self-improvement another thing is exploration um oops and uh one thing I think about is like how can agents learn more about their environment before acting and I work on coding in web agents and there's you know a few good examples of this in in both areas um within coding I view this as like repository understanding understanding the code base that you're dealing with and uh there's an example of this or a couple examples of this one example being agent lists where they basically create a map of the repo and based on the map of the repo they feed that into the agent so the agent can then navigate the repo and uh and better know where things are and for web agents uh there's an example of a paper called bagel and basically what they do is they have the agent um just do random tasks on a website explore the website better understand the structure of the website and then after that they um they feed that in as a part of the prompt um part seven is search um right now in Open Hands we just let the agent go on a linear search path so it's just solving the problem once um we're using a good agent that can kind of like recover from errors and try alternative things things when things are not working properly but still we only have a linear search path but there's also um some nice work in 2024 um that is about exploring multiple paths so one example of this is there's a paper called tree search for language agents and they basically expand multiple paths um check whether the paths are going well and if

they aren't going well you rewind back um and on the web this is kind of tricky because like how do you rewind when you accidentally ordered something you want on Amazon it's kind of you know not not the easiest thing to do for code it's a little bit easier because you can just revert any changes that you made um but I I think that's an interesting topic too and then finally evaluation um So within our development for evaluation we want to do um a number of things the first one's fast sanity checks um and in order to do this we want things we can run really fast really uh really cheaply so um for web we have something called Mini World of bits which is basically these trivial um kind of uh web navigation things um we have something called the AER code editing Benchmark where it's just about editing uh individual files that we use um but we also want highly realistic evaluation so for the web we have something called Web Arena that we created at CMU this is web navigation on real um real open source websites so it's open source websites that are actually used to serve serve um shops or uh like bulletin boards or other things like this um and for code we use sbench which misclick there was this thing about how like claw computer use started uh like looking at pictures of Yellowstone National Park um or something like this I don't know if you heard about this uh the simplest way the second simplest way is uh you take the HTML and you basically identify elements in the HTML um you don't use any Vision whatsoever um and then you say okay I want to click on this element I want to enter text in this element or something like that but HTML is too huge so actually usually gets condensed down into something called an accessibility tree which was made for screen readers for visually impaired people people and um so that's another way and then the Third Way is kind of a hybrid where you present the screenshot but you also present like a a textual summary of the output and that's the one that I think will probably work best um what we're using is we're just using text at the moment and that's just an implementation issue that we haven't implemented the uh intermediate values for the language models is extremely reasonable and it's showing that they're doing something that actually gets to valuable outputs so this is like one of the many ways that we can kind of lead towards 01 is that language models have Randomness built into them and a lot of what people see as failures in reasoning are kind of these language models following very static chains Last week, Curser dropped their own coding model, Composer 2. But unlike other companies, they beat the giants Open AI and Anthropic on both cost and performance. The message was clear. We're not just an AI rapper anymore. We build our own AI models. So, what's the catch? A random developer noticed something odd when poking around Cursor's API. [music] The internal model ID was actually the Chinese open model, Timmy K2.5. So, Cursor didn't create a model from scratch. They used a Chinese-based model with reinforcement learning slapped on top. Creators of the Chinese model eventually called Cursor out for not clarifying. Quickly after, an official partnership with the Chinese company was announced, but the damage was already done. But what's the problem with using an open model? Well, Cursor didn't share this info until they were called out on it. And that's [music] the thing. Cursor's launch wasn't a lie, but it wasn't the whole truth. Users felt deceived even as the results were very impressive. And to win an AI, you must win on trust.

### TestGuild Automation — [TjTygGqP5JQ](https://youtube.com/watch?v=TjTygGqP5JQ)

Kind: captions Language: en Here's the uncomfortable truth about AI and testing that most vendors won't tell you. 99% accuracy sounds great until you realize that just 1% error rate means your entire automation pipeline is untrustworthy. How do I know this? Well, I just spent an hour with Adam Carney from Apply Tools at Automation Guild last month and what he said stop me dead in my tracks. What did he say? Well, let's break it down. So if you don't know, Adam has been in the AI for testing game way before ChachiBT even existed. Way before half these AI powered tools were even founded. Apple tools has been running AI and production test pipelines for almost a decade now. I think the first time I talked to Adam was way back in 2015 on my test guild test talks podcast. But here's what he told me as a tool vendor speaking with all these companies. Teams are being sold a story that doesn't match reality. You know the hype. It all says LLMs will replace testing. But here's what Adam's actually seeing happening at thousands of companies. LLM based test generation developers get maybe 15% production boosts, not the 70% vendors promise. AI agents writing and executing test more like non-deterministic chaos that breaks pipelines and the promise of good enough accuracy. Organizations have zero tolerance for false positives in their regression suites. And this isn't just a theory. This is data from thousands of teams running tests in production. So here's the breakthrough insight Adam shared that actually makes sense. There are two completely different types of AI in testing. You have LLM generative AI and then you have deterministic AI. So LLMs, you know them, you love them, you use them, your chatbts, your Geminis. These are truly amazing for brainstorming test ideas, generating drafts, helping you think, but they're proistic. Every time you run them, you get a different result. That's an awesome feature for creative work, but it's a deal breakaker for automated test execution and quality engineering. Then you have deterministic AI. This is purpose-built AI trained for one specific job. For example, visual validation element detection. It does the same thing every single time. No surprises, no close enough. And here's where teams are really getting this wrong. It drives me crazy. They're trying to use LLMs in places where they

need deterministic AI. And I think my man Adam said it perfectly. Teams expect deterministic, consistent, reliable test execution. The notion of regression testing is something they count on. If a test pass, they know everything is okay. They don't compromise on it. Translation, your automation pipeline isn't a place to experiment with mostly accurate AI. And look, you know me. I am not anti LLM. I use chatpt and claude constantly for test brainstorming, writing docs. Amazing. They help me create little proof of concepts. Awesome. But for actual test execution, CI/CD pipelines, I need to know that if my test pass, they actually passed, not probably passed or 98% sure they passed. Because here's what happens when you trust probabilistic AI in your pipeline. False negatives waste hours debugging phantom bugs. False positives let real bugs ship into production. Your whole team stops trusting your test results. And just like always, once trust is gone, your automation is worthless. Regardless of how much AI you have baked in, you're back to manual testing. And Adam's doing something is really valuable. He's running a webinar on March 25th where he's breaking down exactly where LLMs belong in your testing workflow and where they don't. Even if you miss this or you see this video later on, you could still register down below and get the instant replay. And this is what he told me. He's going to show you a framework for deciding where to use which type of AI, how to avoid common traps teams are falling into right now and what AI you can actually trust looks like in a production pipeline. And if you join Automation Guild, you know this isn't vendor pitching or you heard any of my interviews with Adam. He's always been refreshingly honest about what works and what doesn't. So, if you're a QA lead or engineering manager trying to figure out where AI actually fits in your software development life cycle, this is definitely worth your time. So, make sure to check it out using that special link down below. Even if you can't make the live event, you can still get the replay by registering down below. And if you want to see Adam's full automation guild session where he goes deep on LLM versus deterministic AI distinctions, there's also a link below for that as well. Bottom line, stop

chasing AI trends and start asking what problems am I actually solving and is this the right type of AI for it? And that's how you build testing systems people can trust. And as always, Tarth and keep the good. Catch you here next time. Cheers.

Like we have taken all the scaffolding that has existed in our proprietary repo, spun up a new one, ask codeex with our repo as a reference, write the spec. We tell it, spin up a T-mox, spawn a disconnected codeex to implement the spec, wait for it to be done, spawn another codeex and another T-Mox to review the spec or review the implementation compared to upstream and update the spec so it diverges less. And then you just loop over and over and over. Ralph style until you get a spec that is with high fidelity able to reproduce the system as it is. then there was this but kind of behind it all was this idea of kind of the Unix mindset and the Unix mindset was this thing where basically you don't have these like like in the old days like like the operating system that like made the computer industry really work like in the 1960s was this thing called OS 360 which was this big operating system that IBM developed that was supposed to basically run everything and it was this like giant monolithic architecture in the sky. It was like a you know it was like a giant castle um of software and and by the way it worked really well and they were very successful with it but like it was this huge castle in the sky but it was this thing it was almost unapproachable which is like you had to be kind of inside IBM or very close to IBM and you had to really understand every aspect how the system worked and then the Unix guys originally out of AT&T and then out of out of Berkeley um you know came out and they said no let's have a completely different architecture and the way architecture is going to work is we're going to have we're going to have a prompt and a and a shell and then and then we're going all the functionality is going to be in the form of these discrete modules and then you're going to be able to chain the modules together and so the it's almost like the operating it operating system itself is going to be a programming language. For example, if I was in an organization that is primarily conducting business in North America, I wouldn't want all my quality engineers to be sitting all by themselves offshore somewhere. ### TestGuild Automation — [JxM7dZvBnqE](https://youtube.com/watch?v=JxM7dZvBnqE)

Kind: captions Language: en Is your test coverage keeping pace with your AI accelerated dev team? If the honest answer is no, stay tuned. Can a browser [clears throat] agent beat your entire Selenium test suite for less than a cent per task? We got the benchmark numbers, and plus, are you trusting your AI testing tools a little too much? Find out the answers to all these questions and more in this week's Test Guild News Show for the week of March 29th. So grab your favorite cup of coffee or tea and let's do this. First up is all about a new AI-powered software testing platform called Test GPT from Software Velocity Corp. And it's aimed squarely at the problem that most quality and engineering leaders I talk to are constantly feeling development is accelerating faster than testing can keep up with. But this platform is designed to validate how software is supposed to work, not just what the code happens to do. The distinction is central to what the company is building. Rather than generating tests from source code alone, Test GPT ingests requirements, architecture diagrams, business flows, workflows, and operational constraints to build a system-specific validation model aligned to your users' intended behavior. And they also identify five compounding problems facing modern software testing teams: fragmented requirements, testing bottlenecks, shortened test windows, automation without intent, and disconnected business user insight, each which they argue creates scale risk as AI speeds up the code output. And what I really like is one notable capability addresses a common enterprise reality: when written requirements are insufficient, subject matter experts can be interviewed, and those discussions are then ingested to redefine the system model, allowing testing to proceed based on intended behavior even when documentation is incomplete or evolving. And Test GPT is positioned for complex, fast-changing, and high-risk software, including SaaS platforms, enterprise applications, regulated systems, and mission-critical software where behavior, reliability, and compliance matter. So I highly recommend you check it out using our special link down below. And speaking of tools to help testers, I found another one that's by Petros, who's a test automation lead. He's released an open-source tool called Skynet, a lightweight browser automation helper for testers who need fast UI validation without the overhead of a full framework. Skynet lets you run UI tests directly from the browser console or a bookmarklet. No

installation, no framework, just run. The tool is written in JavaScript and released under the MIT license on GitHub. His cool features include DOM interaction helpers, text and value insertions, checkbox validation, table data validation, visual reporting generation with pass/fail charts, JSON and CSV export, and a recording mode for generating test scripts, screenshot support on failed assertions, and element highlighting. Petros is clear about the scope. Skynet is not a replacement for full end-to-end frameworks like Cypress or Playwright. Instead, it's positioned for exploratory testing, debugging sessions, and quick validations, the kind of work that doesn't always warrant spinning up a full test pipeline. And speaking of open-source tools, one of my favorite open-source contributor, Sai, has just released a new open-source mobile automation agent called called App Claw that has been published by the Appium Test Distribution Project. And it takes a notably different approach to AI-driven mobile testing. Rather than maximizing what the model does, it minimizes how often the model is called. So if you don't know, App Claw is an AI-powered mobile automation agent for Android and iOS that accepts plain English instructions, such as tap, type, swipe, or verify, and figures out the steps to execute them on real devices. And the design philosophy behind it centers on context engineering over prompt engineering. And according to the project's LinkedIn announcement, 30 to 50% of steps are handled locally without any LLM calls. XML page source is compressed from roughly 5 kilobytes down to 3 kilobytes before being sent to the model, and failed actions are fed back directly rather than triggering retry loops. The result, the team claims, is 30-step task completed in under a minute with many individual steps costing zero tokens. And each step, App Claw receives the device screens, reasons by sending the goal and screen state to an LLM to decide the next action, acts by executing that action, and repeats until the goal is complete or the maximized step count is reached. And built-in failure recovery includes stuck detection that injects recovery hints when repeated screens or actions are detected, checkpoints roll back for known good states, and human-in-the-loop pauses for OTTPs, CAPTCHA, and ambiguous choices. All right, another open-source tool I've been hearing more and more about is Aluminum. So this post by Alex, the open-source project Aluminum has reported a new state-of-the-art score on the Web Voyager

benchmark, a standard measure of web browser agent performance. Using Aluminum MCP with Claude Code, the team achieved 98.5 across 610 tasks, surpassing the previous best of 97.1%. And what makes this result really important for testers is the architecture behind it. So rather than building a fully autonomous black box browser agent or flooding a context window with raw browser data, Aluminum sits between those two extremes, exposing a small set of high-level tools that compress browser interactions into a single call. While the main agent stays focused on its context window, it doesn't fill up with browser noise. Three practical conclusions stand out from this report, though. First, the benchmark ran entirely on Selenium, a tool that's been around for 20 years, meaning that there's no need to switch to a modern browser stack to get strong results. Second, Aluminum works primarily with accessibility trees rather than screenshots or pixel coordinates, giving the model more signals at a fraction of the cost. And third, on cost, GPT-5 Nano handles all 610 real-world browser tasks for approximately $5 total, less than a cent per task. Once again, if you mess around with these agents, you can rack up a lot a lot a lot of tokens, and it's just another example as testers how we need to be more and more aware of tokens and tools that could save us token cost. You probably heard of Diffblue, but they just released a new autonomous regression test generation tool that is designed to work along existing AI coding platforms, including GitHub Copilot and Claude Code, to produce verified unit tests across entire codebases without requiring developer intervention. And the benchmark numbers, once again, are the headline here. Across eight real-world Java projects, the Diffblue testing agent delivered an average 81% line coverage and 61% mutation coverage from a single prompt running autonomously. By comparison, an experienced developer spending two hours iterating manually with Claude Code averages just about 32% line coverage and 24% mutation coverage, and that was described in the announcement as a painful process with a rapidly diminishing return past the 50% mark. And the agent handles the full workflow: coverage analysis, built system configuration, test plan creation, parallelization, test generation, output verification, and pull request preparation. Critically, it verifies that every test both compiles and passes, and rolls back any that don't across hundreds or thousands of

classes in a single run. So we talked a lot about AI agents here that are autonomous. How do we handle it? Well, Philip Blue has a great write-up he just posted on LinkedIn. If you don't know, Philip Blue really knows his stuff. He's been a member or board member of PNSQC for years, which leads software quality initiatives. He shares in this article what months of hands-on AI experimentation has taught him about where the technology actually holds up and where it breaks down. And as I said, Philip really knows QA, so you know he's going to give an honest answer to this. His central argument: working well with the AI isn't one skill, it's four. You need to understand the task deeply before you delegate it, which is what testers are good at. Treat prompt crafting as a real skill, recognizing when you're caught in an iterative loop, and know when AI is configured wrong. And none of these, he says, can be learned from reading what to test. Test design is risk reasoning. What could go wrong and what could hurt the user most? What edge cases likely to give how a feature will actually be used? This requires domain knowledge no prompt can replace. And speaking of not being able to trust the output of some of these AIs, especially when it comes to observability and traceability, here's an article I found on LinkedIn by Patrick, where he talks about or makes a case pointing to why trusting AI outputs without understanding how it is produced is a problem testers can no longer ignore. So his argument starts with something testers know well: trust is built through process, not just results. When pairing with another tester, you learn from watching the cursor move and hearing the reasoning follow. The trust is built isn't primarily in the outcome, it's in the coverage and understanding the mental model behind the exploration. LLMs, he says, offers a surface-level version of this. If you ask one to explain itself, you'll get a coherent answer, but the answer is whether that exploration is real reasoning or a post hoc rationalization. The stakes compound in agentic systems, where one agent coordinates, another reviews, several others execute tasks, passing information back and forth. When something goes wrong, there's a real question of where the decisions are actually being made, what agent

introduced the error, and which review step missed it. His conclusion is direct: you can't trace how a decision was made, reproducing the condition that led to it in an output, or identify where to start looking when something fails in production. You're not testing, you're hoping. As testers, we shouldn't be hoping, we should be validating. All right, for links of everything of value we covered in this news episode, head on over to the links down below. So that's it for this episode of the Test Guild News Show. I'm Joe, and my mission is to help you succeed in creating end-to-end, full-stack, pipeline automation awesomeness. And as always, test everything and keep it good. Cheers.
